Header.MessageNote: Seed catalog for development — not a real ONIX feed

Contributor.BiographicalNote: Amara Osei-Mensah was born in Accra and raised in Edinburgh. Her short fiction has appeared in Granta and The White Review.

TextContent.Text: A hypnotic debut novel set between contemporary Edinburgh and 1960s Jamaica. When archivist Vera Mackenzie opens a water-damaged trunk of letters in the National Library of Scotland, she begins a journey that will upend everything she knows about her family — and herself.

Contributor.BiographicalNote: Inês Carvalho is a Portuguese novelist and translator. Her work has been translated into twenty-two languages.

TextContent.Text: Set in Lisbon at the turn of the twentieth century, this sweeping novel follows the illegitimate daughter of a royal cartographer who inherits his life's work — a map of a country that does not exist. Winner of the Costa Novel Award.

Contributor.BiographicalNote: Pete Rawlings is a former detective constable turned crime writer. He lives in West Yorkshire.

TextContent.Text: Detective Sergeant Nadia Okafor is called to a moorland crime scene where three bodies have been arranged in an unmistakeable pattern. As the Yorkshire winter closes in, Nadia realises the killer is one step ahead — and that the past she has spent a decade burying is about to surface.

Contributor.BiographicalNote: Margot Heiselberg is the author of six internationally bestselling thrillers. She divides her time between Hamburg and Tuscany.

TextContent.Text: A retired Interpol agent. A stolen Vermeer. And a confession that arrives forty years too late. John le Carré meets Patricia Highsmith in this taut, elegant thriller set across Amsterdam, Geneva and the Amalfi Coast.

Contributor.BiographicalNote: Kenji Watanabe-Fox holds a PhD in computational linguistics and writes speculative fiction in his spare time. The Silence Engine is his first novel.

TextContent.Text: Far-future Earth. The last engineers of a dying civilisation have built a machine designed to delete language itself — a weapon that makes thought impossible. A linguist, a soldier, and an AI must cross a continent where silence has already fallen to destroy it. A breathtaking debut that asks what it means to be human when the words run out.

Contributor.BiographicalNote: Céleste Renard is a French novelist and screenwriter based in Paris and Montreal. She is the author of the Golem Quartet.

TextContent.Text: In a world where the dead vote and memory is currency, a disgraced senator discovers that someone is stealing the votes of the recently deceased. Epic secondary-world fantasy for readers of Ursula Le Guin and N.K. Jemisin.

TitleElement.Subtitle: How Britain's Clockmakers Won the Race to Map the World

Contributor.BiographicalNote: Dr Helena Marsh is a historian of science at the University of Oxford. She has written for the TLS and the London Review of Books.

TextContent.Text: The gripping history of the Longitude Act of 1714, the clockmakers who competed for its prize, and the sea captains whose lives depended on the winner. A vivid account of science, empire, and obsession.

TitleElement.Subtitle: The Life of Sir William Herschel

Contributor.BiographicalNote: Saul Ingram is the author of several acclaimed biographies of scientists and explorers. He was awarded the PEN Hessell-Tiltman Prize in 2021.

TextContent.Text: A magnificent biography of the musician-turned-astronomer who discovered Uranus, built the largest telescope on Earth, and catalogued the night sky with his sister Caroline by his side. Based on newly uncovered correspondence from the Royal Astronomical Society.

Contributor.BiographicalNote: Tom Birch is a chef and restaurateur. His restaurant The Granary in Ludlow has held a Michelin star since 2019.

TextContent.Text: Sixty recipes that take Sunday lunch seriously again. Built around the doctrine that good fat, good salt, and good time are the only three things a good cook needs. From slow-braised ox cheek to the definitive bread sauce, this is proper British cooking for a new generation.

TitleElement.Subtitle: A Plant-Based Cookbook for Every Season

Contributor.BiographicalNote: Anya Kovalenko is a Ukrainian-Scottish cook and food stylist. She runs the Almanac supper club in Edinburgh.

TextContent.Text: Over 80 vegetarian and vegan recipes organised by season — from spring pea and ricotta bruschetta to a roasted root tray bake that has become legendary at the author's Edinburgh supper club.

Contributor.BiographicalNote: Ruth Holloway is the author of the Luna Fox series and the Starwatch trilogy. She lives in Bath with too many cats.

TextContent.Text: Ten-year-old Mira discovers a small brass fox in her grandmother's attic. When it blinks at her, she realizes this is no ordinary toy — and that the world beneath the city streets is far stranger than she ever imagined. First in the Clockwork Tales series.

TitleElement.Subtitle: A Story of Bravery and Stars

Contributor.BiographicalNote: Marcus Adesanya grew up in Peckham and now lives in Hackney. He works as a secondary school teacher.

TextContent.Text: Fifteen-year-old Sol has always kept her head down. But when her school is threatened with closure, she has to stand up — and discovers the power of her own voice. A warm, urgent YA novel about class, community and the courage it takes to speak.

TitleElement.Subtitle: Why Shallow Work Is Killing Your Best Thinking

Contributor.BiographicalNote: Dr Priya Banerjee is a cognitive scientist at University College London. Her research on attention and deep work has been cited in The Economist and Wired.

TextContent.Text: In an era of constant notification, the ability to think deeply is becoming rare — and therefore increasingly valuable. Combining cognitive science with practical strategy, The Depth Trap is a guide to reclaiming the focused time your best work demands.

TitleElement.Subtitle: A Non-Maths Guide to Data That Matters

Contributor.BiographicalNote: Oliver Grant is a data journalist at the Financial Times.

TextContent.Text: Charts, figures, and statistics are everywhere — and most of them are misleading. This practical handbook teaches you to read data honestly, spot manipulation, and use numbers to make better decisions without needing a maths degree.

TitleElement.Subtitle: The New Science of Your Second Brain

Contributor.BiographicalNote: Professor Leena Harjula holds the Chair in Microbiome Research at King's College London. Her TED talk has been viewed 12 million times.

TextContent.Text: Your gut contains 500 million neurons, communicates with your brain constantly, and may be influencing your mood, your decisions, and your mental health in ways science is only beginning to understand. A revelatory, accessible account of the microbiome revolution.

TitleElement.Subtitle: The Search for What the Universe Is Made Of

Contributor.BiographicalNote: Dr Yusuf Al-Rashid is a particle physicist at CERN and a regular contributor to New Scientist.

TextContent.Text: Ninety-five per cent of the universe is invisible. In this thrilling account of one of science's greatest unsolved mysteries, physicist Yusuf Al-Rashid takes us behind the scenes of the experiments, rivalries and eureka moments in the search for dark matter and dark energy.

TitleElement.Subtitle: How Institutions Lost the Public — and How They Can Win It Back

Contributor.BiographicalNote: Fatima Iqbal is a political scientist at the London School of Economics and a columnist for the Guardian.

TextContent.Text: From parliaments to hospitals to the press, trust in institutions is at its lowest level in recorded history. Drawing on decades of polling data and interviews with politicians, chief executives and activists, this is a clear-eyed diagnosis — and a credible prescription.

TitleElement.Subtitle: A Memoir of Water, Memory and Return

Contributor.BiographicalNote: James Whitmore is a journalist, broadcaster and the author of two previous books. He lives in Shropshire.

TextContent.Text: After his father's death, the author canoes the length of the River Severn, retracing the journeys they took together when he was a boy. Part elegy, part nature writing, part family history — a book about the landscapes we carry inside us.

TitleElement.Subtitle: Reclaiming Your Right to Do Nothing

Contributor.BiographicalNote: Rosie Tanner is a writer and activist who has contributed to the Guardian, Vice and gal-dem.

TextContent.Text: In a culture that treats exhaustion as a status symbol, this manifesto argues for rest as a political act. Drawing on philosophy, neuroscience and the author's own experience of burnout, it offers both a critique and a practical path forward.

Contributor.BiographicalNote: Professor Nora Quinn is Professor of Historical Linguistics at Cambridge. She is the author of The Language of Power and The Last Grammarian.

TextContent.Text: From 'wyrd' to 'selfie', one hundred words trace the full arc of the English language — its Anglo-Saxon roots, Norman conquests, imperial sprawl and digital future. A witty, erudite history that is also a portrait of the culture and people who spoke it.

TitleElement.Subtitle: How to Think Clearly in a Noisy World

Contributor.BiographicalNote: Sarah Cullen spent twelve years as an intelligence analyst before becoming a writer and consultant.

TextContent.Text: A former intelligence analyst explains the frameworks she used to separate signal from noise in high-stakes environments — and how the same techniques can transform decision-making in business and daily life. Available as ebook only.

Contributor.BiographicalNote: Clara Venn is the author of the prize-winning novel The August Letters. She lives in Cornwall.

TextContent.Text: When the world's libraries begin sinking into the sea, a network of archivists builds a fleet of floating reading rooms. A novel about books, memory, and what survives the flood.

Contributor.BiographicalNote: Martin Amis (1949–2023) was one of the most celebrated British novelists of his generation.

TextContent.Text: Martin Amis's savage, brilliant black comedy about love, games, and the end of everything. A murder mystery told backwards, set in a seedy corner of late-twentieth-century London, where barmaid Nicola Six knows she is going to be killed.

Contributor.BiographicalNote: Iain Banks (1954–2013) was a Scottish author of mainstream literary fiction and, as Iain M. Banks, science fiction.

TextContent.Text: Iain Banks's extraordinary debut. On a small Scottish island, sixteen-year-old Frank Cauldhame performs rituals to hold his world in order, while his brother escapes from a psychiatric hospital. Disturbing, darkly comic, unforgettable. (This edition out of print — see current Abacus edition.)

Contributor.BiographicalNote: Sunita Chatterjee is a British-Indian historian and novelist. She is a Fellow of the Royal Society of Literature.

TextContent.Text: Delhi, 1919. Three weeks after the Amritsar massacre, a young Indian civil servant must decide whether to risk everything to expose the truth — or keep his silence and survive. A novel about complicity, courage, and the cost of empire.
